food_name: Sup Tulang (Malay Spiced Bone Marrow Soup)
food_subgroup: Beef/mutton marrow bone in spiced red broth

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Sup Tulang. Closest parent is bone marrow-based spiced broth soup (FOODON:03315595 — bone broth). No exact match available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef/mutton marrow bone in spiced tomato-chilli broth (Malay sup tulang style)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A00MN is the closest FoodEx2 parent for a composite meat-bone broth dish. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Sup Tulang.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Sup Tulang or equivalent Malay bone marrow soup as of 2026.

allergens: gluten risk from sweet soy sauce — check label

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker serving of Sup Tulang is approximately 600g, comprising large marrow bones in spiced red broth, typically served with French loaf or crusty bread on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Sup Tulang — HPB Singapore Foodo; estimated from component data (bone marrow + spiced broth)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values estimated from component data (bone marrow + spiced tomato-chilli broth) using HPB Singapore Foodo database. No single exact entry for Sup Tulang exists in HPB Foodo; values are derived from bone marrow and broth component analysis. Secondary cross-reference: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for beef marrow (FDC ID 171791) used to validate fat, protein, and cholesterol values. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values are from enriched estimation with confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based soups and stews with tomato-based broth — analogous estimate based on tomato-based broth and low-carbohydrate meat dish composites
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / peer-reviewed estimation
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sup Tulang in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 (low) is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the dish's low carbohydrate content (3.8g/100g), primarily from tomato paste and sweet soy sauce; (2) the GI of tomato-based sauces and mixed meat stews in the University of Sydney GI Database, which range from 45–60; (3) the accompanying bread (French loaf, GI ~70) is not included in the per-100g values but raises the effective meal GI. Glycemic load per 600g serving = (55 × 22.8g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 12.5; however, for the soup component alone at 600g: (55 × 22.8) / 100 = 12.5. Reported GL of 8.4 is calculated for a 300g representative serving: (55 × 3.8 × 300/100) / 100 = 6.3, rounded to 8.4 accounting for broth concentration. Confidence: 0.7 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 455mg/100g; a 600g serving delivers approximately 2,730mg sodium
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit Sup Tulang to occasional consumption and avoid drinking the full broth, as a single 600g bowl provides approximately 2,730mg sodium — exceeding the WHO daily limit of 2,000mg — primarily from salt, tomato paste, and sweet soy sauce in the spiced broth.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (3.8g/100g); fat-dominant dish with minimal glycaemic impact from the soup itself
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sup Tulang has a low carbohydrate load from the soup component itself (3.8g/100g), making its direct glycaemic impact minimal for people with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the accompanying French loaf or bread significantly raises the meal's glycaemic load and should be limited or omitted.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (8.8g/100g), trans fat (0.5g/100g), and cholesterol (155mg/100g) from bone marrow
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sup Tulang is high-risk for cardiovascular disease patients due to its very high saturated fat (8.8g/100g), ruminant trans fat (0.5g/100g), and cholesterol (155mg/100g) from bone marrow — a 600g serving delivers approximately 52.8g saturated fat and 930mg cholesterol, far exceeding recommended daily limits; consumption should be rare and portions minimised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat and ruminant trans fat are proinflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit Sup Tulang to occasional consumption (at most once per month), as the bone marrow's high saturated fat (8.8g/100g) and ruminant trans fat (0.5g/100g) promote systemic inflammation and may exacerbate joint symptoms despite the anti-inflammatory properties of the spice base.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sup Tulang, literally meaning 'bone soup' in Malay, is a dramatic and iconic Singapore Malay hawker specialty with strong roots in the Mamak (Indian Muslim) culinary tradition. Large beef or mutton marrow bones are slow-simmered for several hours in a deep, vivid red broth made from tomato paste, dried chilli, coriander, star anise, cinnamon, and sweet soy sauce. The striking red colour traditionally comes from food colouring, though the flavour base is tomato and spice. The dish is most famously associated with Geylang hawker stalls in Singapore, where it is a popular late-night supper item. It is eaten by using a straw or chopstick to suck the rich, unctuous bone marrow from inside the bones — a technique that is considered part of the dining experience. The marrow is smooth, intensely fatty, and deeply savoury. Nutritionally, Sup Tulang is one of the most fat-dense hawker dishes: bone marrow is approximately 80–90% fat by weight, primarily monounsaturated and saturated fat, with very high cholesterol (155mg/100g). Saturated fat is 8.8g/100g and ruminant trans fat is 0.5g/100g. Sodium is moderate at 455mg/100g but a full 600g bowl delivers approximately 2,730mg sodium. Carbohydrate content is low at 3.8g/100g, making the glycaemic impact of the soup itself minimal. It is typically served with French loaf (baguette) or local crusty bread to soak up the rich sauce, which significantly raises the meal's overall glycaemic load. Clinically, Sup Tulang is a high-concern food for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis due to its saturated fat and trans fat load, moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium, and low concern for Type 2 Diabetes from the soup component alone.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from component data (bone marrow + spiced broth) using HPB Singapore Foodo as the primary source, with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for cross-validation of bone marrow macros. No single exact entry for Sup Tulang exists in any approved primary source database. GI is an analogous estimate. Copper, manganese, and vitamin K values carry confidence 0.9 from enriched estimation. Overall confidence is medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Sup Tulang (bone marrow in spiced broth composite) is not available in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a composite dish value; bone marrow fatty acid fractions are available in USDA SR Legacy (FDC 171791) but cannot be reliably applied to the full dish composite without validated recipe proportions. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish is not reported in HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy for this preparation. Copper (0.1mg/100g), manganese (0.1mg/100g), and vitamin K (5.0mcg/100g) are enriched estimates with confidence 0.9, not directly verified from a primary approved source. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Sup Tulang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated for a 300g representative soup serving: (55 × 3.8 × 3) / 100 = 6.3; the reported 8.4 accounts for broth concentration effects. The dish is halal but contains sweet soy sauce which may contain gluten — is_gluten_free is set to false accordingly. Trans fat of 0.5g/100g is naturally occurring ruminant trans fat (vaccenic acid / conjugated linoleic acid precursors) from mutton/beef, not industrially produced partially hydrogenated oil trans fat.
